seoTitle: How to Create Multilingual Reports in Power BI (2026)
metaDescription: Build a single Power BI report that automatically shows different languages using model translation tables and the USERCULTURE() DAX function.
intro: Instead of maintaining separate report files for every language your team needs, Power BI lets you embed translation metadata directly into one model. This guide covers activating localization settings, populating translation tables, and using the USERCULTURE() function so labels and titles adapt automatically to each viewer's locale.

steps.heading: Open the Model view and Properties pane
steps.body: Go to the Model view tab in Power BI Desktop and expand the advanced Culture definitions section in the Properties pane.

steps.heading: Add target languages
steps.body: Use the Add Language option to inject new localization code slots into your model for each language you want to support, such as Spanish or French.

steps.heading: Populate the translation table
steps.body: Export your schema or use a translation management tool to fill in the corresponding localized text strings for each language column.

steps.heading: Build a dynamic title with USERCULTURE()
steps.body: Create a measure using the USERCULTURE() function to detect the viewer's locale and swap displayed titles or labels accordingly.

steps.heading: Test and publish
steps.body: Change your browser locale to simulate different regions, confirm the text swaps correctly, then publish the report to the Power BI Service.

faq.question: Do I need separate report files for each language?
faq.answer: No, the whole point of this approach is to keep one file and let the model's translation tables and USERCULTURE() function handle the language switching.

faq.question: How does Power BI know which language to show?
faq.answer: It reads the viewer's browser or system locale settings and matches them against the language codes you've defined in your model.

faq.question: Can I translate chart titles and table headers, not just text boxes?
faq.answer: Yes, the localization tables can cover table headers, text labels, and chart titles without altering your underlying data queries.

faq.question: What if a language isn't fully translated yet?
faq.answer: Untranslated fields will typically fall back to the report's default language until you complete the corresponding translation row.
